title_en: Marriage and Contracts in Al-Muqaddimaat: Maliki Principles
title_ar: النكاح والعقود في المقدمات: الأصول المالكية
content_en:
Ibn Rushd the Elder's introductory essays on marriage and contracts in Al-Muqaddimaat establish the general principles of Maliki family law and commercial law before the student encounters the extensive and complex discussions in the Mudawwanah. His treatment is notable for its principled approach — explaining not just what the rules are but what purposes they serve and how they derive from the sources.

The marriage introduction begins with the Prophet's statement: 'Marry and multiply, for I will boast of you before the nations on the Day of Judgment' (Ahmad, Ibn Majah). Marriage is not merely a social arrangement in Islamic law but an act of worship when performed with the right intention — fulfilling a prophetic instruction, building a family, and contributing to the Muslim community. Ibn Rushd notes that the legal discussion of marriage should always be understood against this spiritual and social background.

The Maliki conditions for a valid marriage contract are explained systematically: a wali who gives the marriage (not merely the bride's consent expressed through the wali); a specific mahr (the Maliki school requires that a specific mahr be agreed upon, although it can be any lawful property); two upright witnesses; and freedom from any legal prohibition. The Maliki school's requirement that a specific mahr be agreed upon as part of the contract is notable: if no mahr is mentioned, the marriage is not automatically invalid (the woman receives mahr al-mithl), but naming a specific amount is a condition of the most complete form of the contract.

The faskh (judicial dissolution) section is where Maliki family law has been most influential in the modern period. Ibn Rushd explains the principle that a marriage can be dissolved by a judge when it has become harmful to either party, when the husband has failed his fundamental obligations, or when continuation would be unjust. The specific grounds — failure of maintenance, harm, absence, impotence, grave illness, and imprisonment — reflect a legal tradition that understood marriage as a relationship creating mutual obligations, not a one-sided institution.

On the prohibition of riba in commercial contracts, Ibn Rushd introduces the Maliki school's approach to this central prohibition. The Maliki school applies the riba prohibition to all foodstuffs — not only the six items mentioned in the prophetic hadith — based on the rationale that the six items are examples of a general category: foods that are weighed or measured staples. This broader application of the riba prohibition is characteristic of the Maliki school's use of the rationale ('illah) of the prophetic text to extend rulings beyond the literally named items.

The Maliki principle of sadd al-dhara'i' (blocking pretexts to forbidden outcomes) is explained in the contracts context: the Maliki school prohibits not only riba itself but also contracts that, while formally permissible, are designed or likely to function as covers for riba. This preventive principle makes Maliki commercial law somewhat more restrictive than the Hanafi school, which tends to focus more on the formal structure of the contract than on its likely practical use.

Ibn Rushd concludes by explaining the Maliki principle of maslaha mursalah (unrestricted public interest) as a source of law in areas not covered by specific texts. In commercial law, this principle allows Maliki scholars to validate arrangements that serve genuine public needs — agricultural partnerships, irrigation agreements, customary commercial practices — even without a specific prophetic text establishing them, as long as they do not violate any clear prohibition. This flexibility has allowed Maliki commercial law to adapt to different economic contexts across North Africa and West Africa over the centuries.
